# Basic Transitive Resolution
*or what to Do When It Resolves More Than You Declared*

This scenario demonstrates fundamental concept of transitive dependency resolution. It shows how it automatically discovers and includes dependencies of your direct dependencies.

#### Dependency Graph
```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] --> LibA[lib-a:1.0.0]
    LibA --> LibB[lib-b:1.0.0]
    LibB --> LibC[lib-c:1.0.0]
```

#### Gradle (Groovy DSL)

```{.groovy include="/demos/gradle-demo/scenario-1-basic/build.gradle" snippet="s1-deps"}
```

#### Expected Intuition
Developers expect `lib-a`, `lib-b`, and `lib-c` to be present.

#### Actual Resolution Results
Resolves `lib-a:1.0.0`, `lib-b:1.0.0`, and `lib-c:1.0.0`.

#### Classpath Reality
All three JARs are on the classpath.

#### Why This Happens
Gradle traverses the entire dependency graph depth-first, collecting every unique artifact. Each POM or Gradle module metadata file declares its own dependencies, and Gradle follows every edge until it has mapped the complete graph. Since no version conflicts exist in this scenario, every artifact is included at its declared version.

#### How This Scales in Real Systems
In production projects, this simple chain (A → B → C) is replicated thousands of times. A typical Spring Boot application resolves 200--400 transitive dependencies from a handful of direct declarations. The "happy path" of zero conflicts becomes increasingly rare as the graph grows; diamond dependencies emerge naturally.

#### Signals to Watch For
Run ./gradlew dependencies to inspect the full tree. Watch for unexpected artifacts appearing --- a single new direct dependency can pull in dozens of transitives. If you see ClassNotFoundException for a class that should be present, check whether an exclusion or scope/configuration issue is pruning the graph.

#### Key Takeaway
It resolves the full graph by default.

#### Related Scenarios
*   [Scenario 2: Conflict](../scenario-2-conflict/guide.qmd)
